text: This is Dustin Baldwin. Today I'll be discussing a robotic recurrent right inguinal hernia repair. This is a patient who had a bilateral inguinal hernia repair done in the pre-peritoneal plane about a year ago by another provider. He presented to my clinic with complaints of a recurrent right inguinal hernia.
The surgeon examines the surgical site, noting a well-repaired left inguinal area and a moderate-sized direct recurrence on the right side, which is being prepared for repair.

The surgeon begins in the preperitoneal plane, carefully taking down adhesions to access the direct hernia. A piece of pro-grip mesh is visible covering the malpictinial orifice, and the surgeon navigates through significant scar tissue.

The surgeon works medially to reduce the recurrent direct hernia, maintaining the pseudosac or attenuated transversalis fascia. The pro grip mesh is visible, scarred beneath the inferior epigastric vessels, which the surgeon decides to leave untouched for now.

The surgeon works on reducing the direct inguinal hernia, peeling off the contents of the peritoneum and retroperitoneal fat. The attenuated transversalis fascia or pseudosac is visible. The surgeon progresses towards Cooper's ligament, which is scarred from previous dissection, while also addressing adhesions caused by the mesh.

The surgeon carefully dissects around Cooper's ligament, navigating through scar tissue from previous surgeries. The peritoneal sac and transversalis pseudosac are reduced, revealing the underlying anatomical structures. The surgeon works meticulously to stay within the correct surgical plane during this complex redo procedure.
Because of that, I did have to modify my closure, which we'll see later in the case.

The surgeon examines the femoral canal, identifying the femoral vein and avoiding it. The mesh is heavily adhered to surrounding structures, including the epigastric vessels, and the surgeon carefully works laterally to separate the mesh from these vessels.

The surgeon stops the dissection and begins closing the direct defect using a 2-0 V-Loc suture, bringing the tissues together to reinforce the hernia repair after reducing the pseudosac and peritoneum.

The surgeon measures the dissected space, approximately ten by ten centimeters, and cuts a square piece of non-absorbable mesh. The mesh is sutured to Cooper's ligament at two points and also attached to the anterior abdominal wall, covering the direct space.

The surgeon observes that the indirect space is covered by prior mesh and focuses on providing wide coverage over the direct defect. The mesh is sutured to Cooper's ligament with overlap and fixation, extending laterally to the midline and interiorly. The mesh is pushed to cover the femoral space and anchored to the abdominal wall using a running V-lock suture.

The surgeon performs a traditional closure of the flap using a 3-0 V-lock suture. The final view shows the peritoneum closed with the mesh laying flat in good position, without any buckling or wrinkling, and the hernia completely reduced.
